did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Generative Adversarial Networks (specifically Progressive Growth of GANs (PGGAN) and Wasserstein GAN with Gradient Penalty (WGAN-GP)) to generate synthetic melanoma images for data augmentation. It then used a pretrained ResNet50 deep learning model to classify these images (melanoma vs. nevus) from the ISIC 2019 dataset, evaluating classification metrics like precision, recall, and F1-score.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the generation of synthetic melanoma images using PGGAN and WGAN-GP, the application of a dynamic ratio methodology to the ISIC 2019 dataset, and the evaluation of classification performance using a ResNet50 model. It includes specific methodology, results tables, and discussion of new findings, published in the IEEE 38th International Symposium on Computer-Based Medical Systems (CBMS).

title: Assessing a proposed dynamic ratio in dataset class imbalance with GAN’s generated melanoma images
journal: 2025 IEEE 38th International Symposium on Computer-Based Medical Systems (CBMS)
brief_summary: This study addresses class imbalance in melanoma detection by proposing a dynamic ratio methodology for augmenting the ISIC 2019 dataset with synthetic images generated via Progressive Growth of GANs (PGGAN) and Wasserstein GAN with Gradient Penalty (WGAN-GP). The researchers evaluated the impact of varying synthetic-to-real image ratios on classification performance using a pretrained ResNet50 model. Results indicated that specific dynamic ratios improved precision, recall, and F1-score for melanoma detection compared to the baseline, with PGGAN providing superior visual quality. The findings demonstrate that carefully calibrated synthetic data augmentation can mitigate class imbalance issues in imbalanced dermatological datasets.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for synthetic image generation and classification. The study addresses the underrepresentation of melanoma in the ISIC 2019 dataset (4522 images) and generates synthetic melanoma images using PGGAN and WGAN-GP to mitigate class imbalance.

The comparator class in the binary classification task. The study notes that nevus images account for 12875 images in the ISIC 2019 dataset, resulting in an initial imbalance ratio of approximately 2.8:1 (nevus to melanoma).

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into Melanoma and Nevus classes
all_task_types.details: The study uses a pretrained ResNet50 model to classify dermoscopic images into two discrete diagnostic categories: Melanoma and Nevus. The task is evaluated using classification metrics including precision, recall, and F1-score. The model's final layers were adjusted for 2 classes.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration used for skin lesion classification. The study utilized the melanoma and nevus classes from this dataset, which exhibited class imbalance.

use.training: Used as the base dataset for generating synthetic images and creating dynamic ratio test sets (Baseline through Test 5) for classification training and evaluation.
use.validation: Not explicitly reported as a separate split; the study focused on training/testing with dynamic ratios derived from the original dataset counts.
use.testing: Used for evaluating classification performance (precision, recall, F1-score) of the ResNet50 model across different synthetic-to-real image ratios.
use.other: Used as the source for real images to be augmented with synthetic images generated by PGGAN and WGAN-GP.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between the baseline and dynamic ratio tests were made using classification metrics (precision, recall, F1-score) without statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2019 Challenge Dataset.description: Evaluation of ResNet50 classification performance on Melanoma and Nevus classes using synthetic images generated by PGGAN (selected over WGAN-GP for visual quality) added to the dataset at varying ratios.
ISIC 2019 Challenge Dataset.notes: Test 1 and Test 3 surpassed the baseline for Melanoma detection. Test 1 achieved higher precision and F1-score. Test 3 achieved higher recall and F1-score. PGGAN was selected for augmentation as it provided better visual quality (detail in lesion texture and edges) compared to WGAN-GP. Training times for GANs were 7 hours for PGGAN and 9 hours for WGAN-GP on NVIDIA-A4000 GPU. Results are based on 5 epochs of training.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pretrained weights used to accelerate classification results; typical transformations from the official PyTorch documentation were applied.

architecture_details.layers: Not explicitly reported, but standard ResNet50 architecture implied
architecture_details.heads: Final layers adjusted for 2 classes (Melanoma and Nevus)
architecture_details.classifier_head: Adjusted final layers for binary classification (Melanoma vs. Nevus)

classification_or_output_head.details: Final layers of ResNet50 adjusted to align with the study's 2 classes.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Progressive Growth of Generative Adversarial Networks (PGGAN)
preprocessing_techniques: Hair removal using the dull-razor algorithm
Vignette/dark frame removal (elimination of dark frames)
Image resizing (resized synthetic generated images to 224x224 pixels to match ResNet50 input size)

data_augmentation_techniques.technique: Progressive Growth of Generative Adversarial Networks (PGGAN)
data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used to generate synthetic melanoma images for data augmentation. Selected over WGAN-GP due to superior visual quality (detail in lesion texture and edges). Generated images were added to the dataset at varying dynamic ratios (Baseline through Test 5) to mitigate class imbalance.
Used to generate synthetic melanoma images. Evaluated against PGGAN but not selected for final augmentation experiments due to loss of detail in lesion texture and edges compared to PGGAN.
Applied as a traditional image transformation during training with probability p=0.5.
Applied as a traditional image transformation during training with probability p=0.5.

Synthetic PGGAN-generated melanoma images were added to the real ISIC 2019 dataset at specific ratios to balance classes: Test 1 (2.4:1, 843 synthetic images), Test 2 (2:1, 1,073 synthetic images), Test 3 (1.6:1, 1,609 synthetic images), Test 4 (1.2:1, 2,682 synthetic images), and Test 5 (1:1, 2,146 synthetic images).

conclusions: PGGAN provided superior visual quality compared to WGAN-GP, specifically retaining more detail in lesion texture and edges, making it the preferred generator for augmentation.

Synthetic image integration does not guarantee progressive performance improvement; instead, optimal results were achieved at specific dynamic ratios rather than through gradual increases in synthetic data.
Test 1 (ratio 2.4:1 with 843 synthetic images) achieved the highest precision (0.79) and F1-score (0.73) for melanoma detection among the tested ratios.
Test 3 (ratio 1.6:1 with 1,609 synthetic images) achieved the highest recall (0.72) and a competitive F1-score (0.73) for melanoma detection.
Tests 2 and 4 (ratios 2:1 and 1.2:1) resulted in significantly lower recall (0.57) and F1-scores (0.68), indicating that certain synthetic ratios can degrade model performance.
The study demonstrates that carefully calibrated synthetic data augmentation can mitigate class imbalance issues in imbalanced dermatological datasets like ISIC 2019.
Simpler GAN architectures like PGGAN can effectively generate reliable synthetic skin cancer images, challenging the assumption that more complex models are always necessary.
The inconsistency in performance improvements suggests that PGGAN may occasionally generate suboptimal images, potentially due to limitations in training hyperparameters or computational constraints.
Future research is needed to assess the consistency of synthetic images being faithful to the melanoma class to ensure reliable augmentation.
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation, relying solely on the ISIC 2019 dataset, which limits the generalizability of the findings to other clinical populations or imaging devices.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) was performed to validate the observed performance improvements, making it difficult to determine if results are statistically robust.
The classification model was trained for only 5 epochs, which is a very short training duration that may not allow the model to fully converge or generalize effectively.

GAN training was constrained by memory issues, limiting adjustments to hyperparameters such as latent vector size, batch size, and network features, potentially affecting the quality and consistency of synthetic images.

The study did not employ rigorous expert validation for synthetic image quality, relying instead on a 'Visual Turing Test' performed by non-experts, which may not accurately reflect clinical fidelity.

Performance was inconsistent across different synthetic ratios; specifically, Tests 2 and 4 resulted in significantly lower recall (0.57) and F1-scores (0.68), indicating that increasing synthetic data does not guarantee improved performance and can degrade it.
The paper does not report data leakage risks or confirm strict separation of synthetic images from the test set during GAN training, which is critical for unbiased evaluation.

The study focuses only on binary classification (Melanoma vs. Nevus), ignoring other skin lesion types present in the ISIC 2019 dataset, which limits its clinical relevance for broader dermatological diagnosis.
